speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about the trip to Auschwitz from Szebnie-- that people were packed in tight, that the conditions with difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like like cattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone die on the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard a lot of screaming, and yelling, and [NON-ENGLISH]. So that's the last word. So probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a horrible trip. You know, you had to stay. You had no place where to sit. And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] and what can I say? Did they give you food on this trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the train stop at any station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They stopped, and we were-- everything was luck. Nobody was allowed to go out or to come in. It was horrible. I don't have any words for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to see out of any windows?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no windows. There were just only a small window with iron. You couldn't see anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't get close to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't get close to it. Everybody wanted to-- was-- I think it was a good thing. It was very cold, so you were able to breathe, otherwise you would-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Try to ask myself a question. How could human being do something like that to another human being? What happened when you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't criminals. We weren't murderers. We didn't harm anybody. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The crime was, we were born Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got to Auschwitz? What was the first thing you saw when they opened the gate, the doors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it was snow. And the SS used the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] go, go, go, and go. And-- on train-- and next thing I knew, everybody had to-- in five, without shoes, on the snow, barefoot, in socks, everything. And I was there in the line with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they separate you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the two brothers. No-- I was there because we-- we kept together, the three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The women separate, and the men separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. When they came out, the women separate, and the men were separate. And it was Tonia's father and their son, Mayer. In five-- so we-- we tried to be-- I was with my two brothers, and with Tonia's father, and with her brother. And he was that time, I think, 45. He was a young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tonia's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tonia's father. I think because he was in the line with us, they picked him up to the crematorium. Because we were young and he was the oldest. Right away they separate the young people, separate and the older people. The older people, the women, the children. They took right away. The older women, they took right away, to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the separating?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any one person?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was left and right. Left and right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did this? One person, or many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were many people. I don't-- you know, you were in such a shock. You don't know. The yelling, and the screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they yelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Women were screaming. They were separated from the children. Men were screaming. They were separated from their children. It was a horrible scene. You couldn't even think straight. And they didn't give you time to think. They were-- they were beating you. And they-- [NON-ENGLISH], stand up in five. And we come in front of five-- so the four of us, my two brothers and Tonia's brother, they took us, you know, to a different side. And her father, they took right away away. [SOBBING] Such a nice guy. [CRYING] Then they took her. We had to stay in line again, God knows how many hours. And they gave us the numbers. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] And also, a very good friend of mine, in Wisnicz-- which his name is Joe Kuchler. My name is Krieger, his name is Kuchler, so we were, like, together. He was after me. My older brother was first. And then we took a younger brother in the middle. And I was the third one. And he was after us. So I have 161341 and he has 342. He lives in Toronto. We are very good friends. And his son-- and his children and my children are very good friends. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they sent us, which we say in German, to the [GERMAN] to wash-- cold. We had to walk out without wiping ourselves. In that cold weather-- God knows it was winter in Europe, it was cold. I remember snow was there. And they gave us the [NON-ENGLISH], the uniforms. And they took-- took us into the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't remember which one. I remember, from there, we were for a while. And then they took me to the camp, which is Buna. Over there I went in barracks 14. That I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the beginning. What happened in the first days when you were there? What was it like in the first days? What kind of food did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us a soup. Like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a little bit of grass and hot water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: You got the soup once a day, in the evening. In the morning you got some black coffee and a piece of bread. Small slice of bread. Or you gave it twice and lunchtime we got the soup. And at night we got the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make you stand appel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three hours in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was together with you in the lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one are you talking about?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right in the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning? My older brother, my younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mayer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joe Kuchler, and Tonia's brother was there. We like-- we tried to stick together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it help to stick together?

speaker: interviewee
text: It helped. I was there with my brothers for three weeks. That was Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first camp was Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then from there, they sent us in different camps-- Auschwitz. Like, I was sent to Buna. My older brother was sent to [NON-ENGLISH], which was a coal mine. In April 1944, they took him to the crematorium. I got the date. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My younger brother, they sent him to a different camp. And this was the last time when I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first three weeks you were all together. And then you were separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Separated. Yeah, the last time I saw my brothers. [CRYING] [DRINKS] And my sisters went with Tonia. That time she was my wife, already, because when we were in the ghetto, in 1943, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] her father says he thinks we should get married. He had a feeling [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that he's not going to live through the war. That's what he said. So in case she should come out alive-- so he wants somebody should take care of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were married in the ghetto, in April the 6th, 1943. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then we were together until, in the ghetto, until September, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before they sent us to Szebnie. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then when they separated us in Auschwitz, she was together with my sisters. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she told me they all died on typhus. At the same time? Was there an epidemic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We made up-- if you'll come out alive, we should meet in Bochnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you discuss this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they separated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a chance to talk before you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I came-- I was liberated by Schindler. Before we do that, let's talk a little bit more about Auschwitz. In those first three weeks, when you were together with your brothers and Mayer, and your friend, were you given work? Did you have work to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We used to carry stones, about two or three kilometers. I must have been young and strong, because a lot of people died doing that job. They gave us big stones to carry one way. And then the other people carried the same stones back, like a torture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: For no reason at all. With the German Shepherds. And if you, God forbid if you dropped the stone-- if you didn't hold it, if you didn't carry it, the German Shepherds ate you up alive. That was a horrible three weeks. Then when they sent us away, already-- Buna, it wasn't-- comparing what it was, it was much better there. But it's nothing to talk about. It's-- I shouldn't use that word, it was better. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there. And worked to unload railroads. Used to come in with coal. Used to-- In Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buna. We used to unload [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] pipes, I remember-- tremendous big pipes. They used to bring-- it was [NON-ENGLISH] farm industry, they were building there. We used to carry those pipes. Unload them, and load them. It was terrible. Outside-- snow, rain, hungry, without food, without everything. And they worked like that until February 21st, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They selected 500 people. And I couldn't walk anymore. I couldn't carry anymore. So, I was very weak, so they took me to the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is a [NON-ENGLISH], like a hospital. And every day they picked up 500 people. As many people as-- you know, from that section, to the crematorium, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was picked up. I was between the 500 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: On February 21st?

speaker: interviewee
text: On-- on my birthday. That's the reason I remembered. And I said, what a day that they picked-- we knew that we were going to the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the Germans kept papers for the Red Cross, so they had 30 people a month. They picked up from those 500 people to peel potatoes in the kitchen, where they cooked for the SS. And I was the lucky one. I was picked from the 500 people. I was between the 30 people, what they picked me out-- because I was all swollen. And I kept myself clean, and-- so they only looked at your body, and at your fingers. If you don't have any rash. If you had a rash, they didn't-- right away, you weren't qualified to go there. And I was lucky. I was-- I didn't have a rash. And I was swollen, so I looked like a healthy guy. And I was picked between the 30 people. And they brought me into the kitchen there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the kitchen like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a kitchen. A big kitchen. We peeled potatoes. They had what they cooked for the SS, what there was guarding the camp. Then they gave us-- they asked, who is a tailor? I was a tailor, so I said, I'm a tailor. There were other, seven, eight guys-- I don't remember how many. I know it was around seven or eight guys. And they gave us two pails of hot water to carry from the kitchen to the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is a laundromat where they washed the clothing, the shirts for the SS, the jackets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- God wanted me to survive. That's the only [SOBBING] thing what I could figure out. I was lucky that day. And I came in-- the first one with the two pails of water, so I was picked. Because every 30 days, they changed a person in that [NON-ENGLISH], like they change 30 people in the kitchen to peel potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were five steady guys, and every month, they changed one guy. And I came in there. That's a happy note-- it's like hell and heaven. Outside was hell, inside there was heaven. I saw bread-- and a guy just walked in from the kitchen with a pail of soup. And I remember that smell until today. I didn't see that-- not in the ghetto, not in-- not in Auschwitz. And it was a guy-- he was the head. His name was Kurth Fondel. And he looks at me, and he says to me, if you want to live, don't touch anything what's in here. We can hide for you-- they had salami, they had bread, they had butter, they had soup-- they had everything there. Like, as I said, outside was hell. In there was heaven. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I looked at him, and he says, I'm telling you again. If you want to live, don't touch anything. I said, what are you talking about? You know, in German. So he says to me, your stomach is not eat-- is not used to food anymore. Once you gonna touch that fat stuff, you're gonna-- excuse me-- you're going to get the diarrhea, and the next day, you're gone. That's what's happened here. And I looked at him-- to have a willpower like that, and I could say only one thing, that God, or [NON-ENGLISH] and gave me the willpower to listen to him. That's all what you had to do, was listen to him. And he said to me-- I said to him, Kurth, who doesn't want to live? Of course I want to live. So he said, if you want to live, listen to me, and you're going to live. And I listened to him. He went out with me, and he took a potato, and he cut it into four pieces. And he made, like you say in America, baked potato. But it was dark-- well done. So until today, everything that I eat, I like it well done.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he burned that potato. He came inside and gave me a cup of black coffee. And he said to me, eat this up. I know you're hungry, but in a half an hour or an hour, he said, I'll give you another portion. And if you're going to eat this for a few days, he said, you'll be OK. And that's what he did. He took a piece of bread after a few hours, and he made a toast. And he gave me black coffee. And eat. He said, if you're going to eat this, your stomach is going to get used to food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know what? After two weeks, I was completely a new person. I was healthy. I was strong. Where did you sleep while you were in the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: They had their own sleeping quarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had their own. We didn't have to go to an appel in the morning. We slept in a beautiful barracks with all the big shots, which the head of the camp, which it was called the lagerfuhrer. He was sleeping over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the lagerfuhrer?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German guy. He was in charge. He had political heft. And he was like a politician. When I came in there--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was also a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the upper echelon of prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in there in that block, and they gave me a blanket to cover myself, and a white sheet to sleep on it. I said, God, I must have done something good in my life, you know, to deserve that. And I was there. And it was just, after I was there for two weeks, I said to the four or five guys, I don't want you to do anything. I'm going to wash, I'm going to iron, I'm going to fix-- I'm going to do everything. I said, I don't want you-- I'm going to work for all five of us. And they all happened to like me. But I knew, after four weeks, that this-- all the good things come to an end. So on the third week, the fourth week-- I go over to Kurth, and I said to him, Kurth, I want to talk to you. And I said, I have a question. So remember, I said, I'm going to ask you for something. And I go to him, and he said, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, do you want me to live? He says, remember when you said to me, the other way around? And I said, Kurth, if you want me to live, if you're going to send me outside, I'm never going to live. Because when I was outside, I made up my mind. I'm not better than the next person. I'm young, I'm strong-- as long as I was able-- because I saw all my friends, my brother, and my closest friends, they died on me. After two months, three months. You saw Mayer die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Mayer? What happened to Mayer, you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer, they sent away in a different camp. I didn't see him in Auschwitz anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said your brother-in-law. Which brother-in-law was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's husband-- Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pincusfeld, . Shlomo Pincusfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very well off at home, you know. And all those guys, what they had good at home, they came to Auschwitz, and they lasted a month, or two, or three, at most.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because they weren't used to such a hard labor, and such conditions and everything. I worked hard all my life, since I was 10 years old. So probably that helped me a lot, to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Pincusfeld?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you there when it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked the same work what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lasted till-- till February 1944. He went to the end of 1943. Never made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: He never made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he killed, or did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he died. He wasn't killed. They worked them to death, you know? He couldn't-- he couldn't work anymore. That's it. They took him to the [NON-ENGLISH]. The next thing, it was the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was selected for the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. If you couldn't go to work anymore. [DRINKS] So many of my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were a lot of people that you knew there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Because we tried to stick together-- you know, from the same town. To help each other, you know, whoever. I was able to help after I was in that [NON-ENGLISH]. When I said to him, the fourth week, now, if you're going to send me out, you're going to have me on my-- your conscience, because I'll never make it. If I didn't know that something like this exists-- I said, whatever happened to everybody's going to happen to me. But if I know something like this exists, I said, my hope is gone. And he tried, and he tried, and he said, I promise you, I'll do everything that I can to take you in as a sixth person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job in the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did everything. I washed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you washing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The clothes for the SS. You know, the shirts, the jackets that they wore. Not the uniforms, but the hats from the kitchen. They wore white jackets every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do I know that my brother died February-- I mean, April 24, 1944? Kurth took me in as a fourth-- sixth person in the [NON-ENGLISH]. I started up and I didn't finish. I jumped one to another.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was there, I come into the kitchen, and I see-- the German law was that every second day, you had to give them a white jacket. I walked into the kitchen once, and I said to obersturmfuhrer, give me your jacket. I don't want to speak German, because I hate it. Give me your jacket. I'm going to give you a new one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, no, no-- not today. Tomorrow. I says, no. I said, it's not nice that you should walk around with that dirty jacket. He looked at me like this. And he takes off his jacket, and he says, come with me. I got scared already.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go with him. He went into the warehouse-- a warehouses is the magazine, what they had-- in German is-- in English, the warehouse, where they had all the food and everything. He took two salamis like that. He wrapped it around in his jacket. And he gave it to me. He says, you have a lot of friends outside, which they are hungry. He said, give it to them. And he goes outside. But there was a guy standing outside there. So he says to him-- because he was in charge, he was the obersturmfuhrer-- so he says to him, that me, he doesn't have to check, because I take out his stuff-- the stuff what I'm carrying is his clothing. And he says to me, don't worry about it. If he'll-- if he'll check you, he has to bring you to me. So nothing is going to-- I said, I'm not going to take anything. If you'll give me, I'll take. If you don't give me-- and I became so friendly with him because of that, he used to give me a radio into the [NON-ENGLISH] to listen to the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember. I don't remember his name, but I was looking for him. He was in the SS. The SS obersturmfuhrer [NON-ENGLISH]. So he said, he had the news that his wife and two children got bombed and they got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a pause now and continue on the next tape.
